location: Visakhapatnam Steel Plant, Coke Oven Battery No. 4, Andhra Pradesh
root_causes: Entrapped coke oven gas in the battery flue system due to inadequate purging
Hot work permit issued in Zone B while gas pressure sensor readings were elevated
Failure of intelligence layer to correlate gas sensor data with active work permits
Shift changeover during maintenance activity — communication gap between outgoing and incoming crew
Manual handoff process between safety SCADA and permit-to-work system
oisd_violations: OISD-116 Section 5.3: Hot work permit issued with LEL reading above 20% threshold
OISD-116 Section 8.1: Gas testing not performed within 30 minutes prior to confined space entry
Factory Act 1948 Section 38: Explosive atmosphere safety procedures not followed
lessons_learned: Gas sensor data existed and warning thresholds were breached 47 minutes before the explosion. No automated correlation system existed to connect the elevated readings to the active hot work permit in the same zone. The incident demonstrates the critical need for compound risk detection — no single data point was sufficient to prevent the event, but the combination of elevated CH4, active permit, and shift changeover created a critical risk window that went undetected.
content: On January 13, 2025, at approximately 14:23 IST, an explosion occurred at Coke Oven Battery No. 4 of the Visakhapatnam Steel Plant operated by RINL in Andhra Pradesh, killing eight contract workers and injuring eleven others. The workers were engaged in maintenance activity on the battery's gas collector main under a hot work permit (PTW-2025-0089) issued by the day shift safety supervisor. Post-incident investigation revealed that the plant's gas pressure sensors in Zone B had been recording readings above the 25% LEL warning threshold since 13:36 IST — 47 minutes before the explosion. However, no automated system existed to correlate these elevated readings with the active hot work permit or the simultaneous shift changeover occurring at 14:00 IST. The investigation by DGFASLI found three simultaneous compound risk factors: elevated CH4 at 31% LEL, an active hot work permit in the same zone, and a crew handover that created a 23-minute window of reduced supervision. The OISD-116 standard explicitly prohibits hot work permits when LEL exceeds 20%, but the permit was issued before the gas levels rose and was not reviewed when conditions changed. Eight families lost their breadwinners in a preventable incident.
source: DGFASLI Investigation Report 2025, The Wire Investigation Jan 2025, Ministry of Labour Press Release

root_causes: Gas leak from corroded pipeline section during maintenance
Confined space entry without continuous gas monitoring
No atmospheric testing before entry — last test was 2 hours prior
Emergency response delayed by 18 minutes due to unclear evacuation protocols
oisd_violations: OISD-118 Section 7.2: Continuous atmospheric monitoring not maintained during confined space work
OISD-105 Section 4.1: Pre-entry atmospheric testing requirement not met
Factory Act 1948 Section 36: Safe means of entry and exit not provided
lessons_learned: The gas concentration in the confined space had risen to dangerous levels between the last manual gas test (2 hours before entry) and the time of entry. Continuous monitoring would have detected this change. Confined space entry must be supported by real-time atmospheric monitoring, not periodic manual checks. The 18-minute emergency response delay cost one life — the third fatality occurred during evacuation due to secondary smoke inhalation that could have been avoided with faster response.
content: On June 18, 2023, a fire occurred at ONGC's Uran processing plant when three workers entered a confined space (a process vessel undergoing maintenance) without verifying current atmospheric conditions. The last gas test had been conducted two hours earlier and showed safe levels. During the intervening period, a corroded section of an adjacent hydrocarbon pipeline had been leaking, raising the hydrocarbon concentration inside the vessel to 38% LEL. When one worker used an electric tool, it triggered ignition. The fire spread rapidly in the oxygen-enriched confined space. Emergency services were delayed because the plant's evacuation alarm was triggered manually, not automatically, and the duty safety officer was in a meeting in an adjacent building. DGFASLI investigators noted that a continuous monitoring system connected to an automated alert mechanism would have detected the rising hydrocarbon levels and prevented entry. Post-incident, ONGC retrofitted real-time atmospheric monitors in all confined spaces at the facility.
source: DGFASLI Annual Report 2023-24, ONGC Safety Bulletin 2023

root_causes: Blast furnace gas (BFG) leakage from expansion joint during simultaneous maintenance activities
Two work permits active in adjacent zones — cross-zone risk not assessed
CO gas detectors in Zone 3 were under scheduled maintenance — gap in monitoring coverage
Workers lacked real-time CO exposure information
oisd_violations: OISD-116 Section 6.4: Simultaneous operations safety assessment not performed
OISD-116 Section 9.2: Backup monitoring required when primary detectors are offline
DGMS Circular 2020-11: Personal gas monitors required for all BFG exposure zones
lessons_learned: The fatalities resulted from CO poisoning from blast furnace gas leakage. The critical failure was that two maintenance permits were active in adjacent zones and no system assessed the combined risk. When the Zone 3 CO detectors went offline for scheduled maintenance, no compensatory monitoring was put in place. A digital permit intelligence system cross-referencing active permits against monitoring coverage gaps would have flagged this dangerous condition before work began.
content: On September 7, 2022, two workers at JSPL Raigarh died from carbon monoxide poisoning in the blast furnace area. They were working under a cold work permit in Zone 3 while a separate team was performing welding under a hot work permit in adjacent Zone 4. The welding activity caused thermal expansion that opened a hairline crack in an expansion joint in the BFG distribution line. CO from the BFG began accumulating in Zone 3. The permanent CO detectors in Zone 3 were offline for their scheduled 6-month calibration — a fact known to safety staff but not cross-referenced against the active permit in that zone. Workers had no personal CO monitors. Two workers collapsed within minutes of CO reaching dangerous concentrations. Their colleagues, unaware of the hazard, initially tried to assist them and were themselves exposed. The incident highlighted the danger of monitoring coverage gaps coinciding with active work permits in the same zone.
source: DGFASLI Investigation Report 2022, JSPL Safety Committee Report

facility: Steel Authority of India Limited — Bhilai Steel Plant
root_causes: Hydrogen gas accumulation in enclosed switchgear room
Electrical work permit active during abnormal H2 accumulation event
Ventilation system failure not reflected in permit system
Night shift with reduced supervision — 3 AM incident
oisd_violations: OISD-116 Section 5.3: Electrical work in classified hazardous area during active gas accumulation
Factory Act 1948 Section 36: Adequate ventilation not ensured before electrical work
OISD Standard 118: Flammable atmosphere check not performed before energising equipment
lessons_learned: The ventilation system failure in the switchgear room occurred 2 hours before the explosion but was not logged in the permit system. The electrical work permit remained active despite changed conditions. An automated system that tracks equipment status changes and cross-references them against active permits would have identified the dangerous condition. Night shift incidents are disproportionately represented in steel plant fatality data — reduced supervisor presence requires stronger automated safety systems.
content: On November 22, 2023, at 03:17 IST, an explosion in the switchgear room of Bhilai Steel Plant's coke oven area killed five electrical workers and injured nine. The workers were performing panel maintenance under an electrical work permit. Unknown to them, the room's mechanical ventilation system had failed at approximately 01:00 IST, allowing hydrogen gas (produced by the battery charging systems) to accumulate. By 03:00 IST, H2 concentration in the room had reached 31% LEL. When an electrical arc was created during panel work, it ignited the accumulated gas. Investigators found that the ventilation failure had been noticed by an operator who logged it in the mechanical maintenance system, but no interface existed between the mechanical system and the permit-to-work system. The electrical work permit continued as valid despite the changed safety conditions.
source: DGFASLI Investigation Report 2024, SAIL Safety Board Review

root_causes: Hot work near uncleaned hydrocarbon residue in draining vessel
Last hydrocarbon content check was 72 hours prior — not re-verified before hot work permit issuance
Permit system did not require re-verification of gas-free conditions after 24-hour intervals
oisd_violations: OISD-105 Section 5.1: Gas-free certificate must be re-issued if 24 hours have elapsed since last check
OISD-116 Section 5.3: Hot work permit requires fresh atmospheric test on day of work
Factory Act 1948 Section 38: Explosive atmosphere work safety requirements
lessons_learned: The hot work permit was valid but the gas-free certificate it relied upon was 72 hours old. OISD-105 requires re-testing within 24 hours. The permit system did not have any automated check for certificate expiry. A digital permit intelligence system that validates permit conditions against regulatory requirements (including time-bounded certificates) would have caught this gap.
content: On March 14, 2024, a flash fire at NALCO's aluminium smelter in Angul killed one contract worker and injured four during hot work on a process vessel. The vessel had been drained and declared gas-free 72 hours earlier, but residual hydrocarbon condensate had accumulated in a low-lying section. A hot work permit was issued based on the 72-hour-old gas-free certificate without fresh atmospheric testing. When welding began, the heat caused the condensate to vaporize, reaching explosive concentration within seconds. Investigators found that no automated system existed to flag expired gas-free certificates at the point of permit issuance.

root_causes: BFG pipe rupture during pressure test — simultaneous activity not flagged
Three separate work teams in adjacent zones without cross-zone visibility
CO concentration exceeded 1000 ppm within 4 minutes — no personal alarms triggered
oisd_violations: OISD-116 Section 6.4: Simultaneous operations in adjacent zones require combined safety review
DGMS Circular: Personal gas monitors mandatory for all BFG work zones
OISD-116 Section 8.3: Pressure testing requires exclusion zone establishment
lessons_learned: Three separate work permits were active in adjacent zones. None of the three safety supervisors had visibility into the others' activities. When a BFG pipe ruptured during a pressure test in Zone 5, CO spread rapidly into adjacent Zones 4 and 6 where workers were active. A compound risk detection system with cross-zone permit visibility would have identified the dangerous simultaneous operations pattern before work began.
content: On April 9, 2022, four workers at Tata Steel Jamshedpur died from CO poisoning following a BFG pipe rupture. Three separate maintenance permits were active in adjacent zones (4, 5, and 6) of the blast furnace area. Zone 5 team was conducting a hydraulic pressure test on a repaired BFG line. The pressure test caused a failure at an adjacent junction, releasing a large volume of BFG. CO concentrations in Zones 4 and 6 exceeded 1000 ppm within 4 minutes — a concentration that causes loss of consciousness within minutes. Workers had no personal CO monitors. The rapid onset left insufficient time for manual evacuation.
source: DGFASLI Annual Report 2022-23, Tata Steel Safety Review

root_causes: Oxygen-deficient atmosphere in reactor vessel — O2 at 14% (minimum safe: 19.5%)
Confined space permit issued without checking vessel purge completion
Standby person left the work site during the entry — violation of permit conditions
No continuous O2 monitoring inside confined space during work
oisd_violations: OISD-116 Section 8.1: Confined space atmosphere must be verified before entry and continuously during work
OISD-116 Section 8.5: Standby person must remain at entry point throughout confined space work
Factory Act 1948 Section 36: Oxygen deficiency is a notifiable hazardous condition
lessons_learned: All three workers died from oxygen deficiency. The confined space permit had been issued assuming the nitrogen purge was complete, but the vessel still contained nitrogen at a level that had displaced oxygen. Continuous O2 monitoring inside the space would have detected the dangerous atmosphere before workers became incapacitated. The standby person's absence removed the last line of defence. Digital permit systems must track standby person assignment and flag when continuous monitoring is not active.
content: On August 30, 2023, three workers entered a reactor vessel at Hindalco Renukoot under a confined space permit. The vessel had been purged with nitrogen for cleaning. The purge was believed to be complete but continuous O2 monitoring was not conducted inside the space. The first worker to enter collapsed within 90 seconds at the bottom of the vessel. Two colleagues descended to assist and also collapsed. All three died from oxygen deficiency — the atmosphere inside was 14% O2 against the minimum safe threshold of 19.5%. The standby person, who should have remained at the entry point to raise the alarm, had stepped away for a tea break. Emergency services were called by a passing worker 12 minutes after the first collapse.

root_causes: Chlorine gas release from corroded valve during maintenance
No real-time Cl2 sensor coverage in the affected maintenance bay
Emergency response team reached the site 11 minutes after the release began
Workers not equipped with SCBA — only half-face respirators available
oisd_violations: OISD Standard 144: Continuous chlorine monitoring required in all chlorine handling areas
Factory Act 1948 Section 41B: Hazardous chemical facilities must maintain emergency response readiness
OISD Standard 105: Personal protective equipment specification for chlorine handling
lessons_learned: The chlorine release occurred from a valve bonnet failure during maintenance. No Cl2 sensor was installed in the maintenance bay — sensors were only at fixed process locations. An 11-minute emergency response delay was primarily caused by lack of automated alerting. The incident demonstrates that sensor coverage gaps in maintenance areas are a common and deadly blind spot. Work permit systems should require sensor coverage verification as a precondition for maintenance in chemical handling areas.
content: On July 19, 2024, two workers died and 18 were injured at Grasim's chemical plant in Nagda when a corroded valve bonnet failed during maintenance, releasing chlorine gas. The maintenance bay had no fixed Cl2 sensors — monitoring was installed at process equipment locations but not in maintenance areas. Workers smelled chlorine but the concentration rose quickly before they could evacuate. The emergency team, equipped with SCBA, arrived 11 minutes after the first alarm was raised manually. The two fatalities occurred nearest to the release point. Investigation found that the maintenance work permit did not require verification of sensor coverage in the work area.
source: DGFASLI Investigation Report 2024, Madhya Pradesh Factory Inspectorate

facility: Indian Oil Corporation Limited — Mathura Refinery
root_causes: Hydrocarbon vapour accumulation in a low-lying area during crude distillation unit maintenance
Hot work permit for welding activity 35 metres away — deemed safe by manual assessment
Wind direction change brought vapour cloud to ignition source
Vapour dispersion modelling not used during permit assessment
oisd_violations: OISD-118 Section 5.2: Hot work exclusion zone calculation must account for wind direction variability
OISD-105 Section 6.1: Vapour cloud dispersion modelling required for hot work near hydrocarbon sources
Factory Act 1948 Section 38: Explosive atmosphere controls
lessons_learned: The hot work permit was issued at 35 metres from the open drain — deemed adequate under static conditions. When wind direction shifted, the vapour cloud reached the welding area within 4 minutes. Real-time meteorological integration with permit systems would have flagged the changing risk as wind direction rotated. This incident demonstrates why permits must be dynamically re-evaluated against changing environmental conditions, not just assessed at issuance.
content: On May 26, 2021, six workers were killed and 22 injured in an explosion at IOCL Mathura Refinery during maintenance of the crude distillation unit. A hot work permit for welding had been issued for a location 35 metres from an open process drain. At the time of permit issuance, the hydrocarbon vapour cloud from the drain was drifting in the opposite direction. Approximately 90 minutes after work began, wind direction shifted and carried the vapour cloud toward the welding area. The vapour ignited on contact with the welding arc. The resulting VCE (Vapour Cloud Explosion) caused damage across a 200-metre radius. Investigators noted that real-time wind monitoring data was available from the plant's weather station but was not integrated with the permit system.
source: OISD Investigation Report 2021, IOCL Safety Board

facility: Hindustan Petroleum Corporation Limited — Mumbai Refinery
root_causes: Near-miss: Flash fire during tank cleaning operation
Flammable vapour concentration not adequately reduced before hot work commenced
Permit system approved work based on a single LEL reading at the tank opening — not inside
Sensor positioned incorrectly — did not detect vapour accumulation in low-lying tank corners
oisd_violations: OISD-105 Section 5.2: Multiple sampling points required for large confined space clearance
OISD-116 Section 5.3: Gas testing must be performed at the point of proposed hot work, not at entry points
OISD Standard 112: Tank entry and cleaning safety procedures
lessons_learned: No fatalities in this near-miss, but seven workers sustained burns. The LEL measurement was taken at the tank hatch (the highest point) while flammable vapour had accumulated in the tank corners and low-lying areas. OISD-105 requires multiple sampling points for confined space clearance. This near-miss demonstrates how single-point sensor readings can create a false sense of safety in large enclosed spaces. Digital permit systems should enforce multi-point measurement requirements before approving confined space hot work.
content: On February 11, 2023, a flash fire injured seven workers during tank cleaning operations at HPCL Mumbai Refinery. The workers were inside a large storage tank that had been declared gas-free based on a single LEL reading taken at the tank hatch. Flammable vapour from residual hydrocarbon sludge had accumulated in the tank's low-lying corners where ventilation was less effective. When a worker's electrical lamp malfunctioned and arced, it ignited the accumulated vapour. The fire flashed across the tank interior in seconds. All seven workers sustained burns ranging from minor to serious. No fatalities occurred due to the proximity of the tank entry manhole and rapid evacuation. OISD-105 requires multiple sampling points for confined space clearance, a requirement that was not met.
source: DGFASLI Near-Miss Report 2023, HPCL Safety Bulletin
